title: Piano hinge binding
subtitle: A post-and-loop binding that opens completely flat at every page
excerpt: Piano hinge binding links a stack of single sheets through a row of loops punched near the spine edge. The loops of adjacent pages interlock and a metal rod passes through the full stack. The result opens flat at any page and allows sheets to be added or removed.
sourceNotes: Piano hinge binding is a modern artists'-book structure developed in the second half of the twentieth century. Technique synthesised from general hand-bookbinding practice and the published artists'-book literature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A post-and-loop binding structure in which loops cut or punched near the spine edge of each sheet interlock with the loops on adjacent sheets. A rod threaded through the full stack acts as the hinge pin.

content.text: takes its name from the continuous hinge along the edge of a piano lid. Each page has a row of loops punched or cut along one edge; these loops interlock with the loops of neighbouring pages, and a rod through the centre holds the whole stack in register. You can add or remove pages without unthreading the rod, which makes it useful for sketchbooks and working reference books that grow over time.

attrs.title: Safety: cutting tools
attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt blades. Always cut against a metal ruler on a self-healing mat. Change blades at the first sign of dulling.

items.name: Paper for pages
items.description: Individual sheets, not folded into signatures. 90-160 gsm depending on use. All sheets cut to the same size.

items.name: Cover card
items.description: Two pieces of 2 mm bookbinding board (slug: bookbinding-board-2mm), cut to the same height as the pages. The boards get the same loop pattern as the pages.

items.name: Metal rod
items.description: A knitting needle, a thin dowel, or a length of 4 mm steel rod, cut to the height of the book block minus 5 mm. Stainless steel or brass for a long-life binding; a wooden dowel for a lighter result.

items.name: Craft knife (slug: craft-knife)
items.description: With a fresh No. 11 blade for cutting the loop slots.

items.name: Bookbinding awl (slug: bookbinding-awl)
items.description: For marking and starting the slot cuts.

items.name: Bone folder (slug: bone-folder)
items.description: For folding the spine-edge tabs over.

content.text: Mark the loop slot pattern on a template sheet. Each slot is 12 mm long and cut 4 mm from the spine edge. The slots alternate: on odd-numbered pages the first slot begins 6 mm from the head and continues every 24 mm down the spine; on even-numbered pages the first slot begins 18 mm from the head. When odd and even pages are interleaved, the tabs from each page fill the gaps of its neighbour, forming a continuous interlocking chain.

Stack all pages in order over the template and punch position marks through the stack with the awl. Then cut the slots individually: hold each sheet on the cutting mat and make two parallel cuts 12 mm long, each 4 mm from the spine edge, separated by a 6 mm land at each end of the slot. Fold the cut tab toward you and burnish flat with the bone folder.

Lay page 1 flat with tabs folded away from you. Slide page 2 so its tabs go through the slots of page 1. Check that the interlocking is clean across the full height. Continue adding pages in order, alternating the tab direction with each new sheet. Add the cover boards last at front and back; their loop pattern matches the odd-numbered pages.

Once all sheets are interleaved, thread the rod through the full stack from head to tail. The rod should pass cleanly through the continuous channel formed by the interlocking tabs. If it catches, check for a misaligned tab and re-fold before threading further.

For a metal rod, file or grind the cut ends smooth so they do not snag. A small drop of beeswax on each end prevents rust on a steel rod. For a dowel, apply two coats of beeswax and polish; this allows the rod to slide out for page additions. Wrap the exposed rod section at head and tail with a strip of book cloth if a neat finish is needed.

items.symptom: Rod will not thread through the full stack
items.cause: One or more tabs folded in the wrong direction, blocking the channel
items.fix: Slide the rod in as far as it will go and examine the first blocked tab. Re-fold in the correct direction. Work through any blocked tabs before continuing.

items.symptom: Pages slip out of register vertically
items.cause: Slots not cut at consistent heights across the stack
items.fix: Use the template punch method to mark all sheets from a single template before cutting individual slots.

items.symptom: Tabs tear at the fold when opening
items.cause: Paper too light for the slot width, or fold not burnished sharply enough
items.fix: Use 120 gsm or heavier paper for the pages. Burnish each tab fold firmly with the bone folder before assembly.
